brands.name: Dog Haus
brands.notes: Pasadena, CA-founded craft hot dog, sausage, and burger fast-casual/biergarten brand (Dog Haus Worldwide), franchising since 2013 with roughly 59 US locations and a large signed development pipeline (100+ units). Multiple build formats: fast-casual restaurant, full biergarten, and lower-cost remote/ghost kitchen. Actively recruiting US franchisees via doghaus.com/franchise.

riskFlags.title: Negative members' equity disclosed in FDD financials
riskFlags.detail: Dog Haus's audited financial statements show a members' deficit of $(1,452,363) in 2024 and $(2,019,611) in 2023. The Hawaii state addendum notes the estimated initial investment exceeds the franchisor's equity (a deficit), a standard regulatory flag that the franchisor may have limited financial resources to support franchisees.
riskFlags.source: Dog Haus 2025 FDD financial statements (via Vetted Biz FDD analysis)

riskFlags.title: Pipeline (100+ units) is roughly double the open base (~59)
riskFlags.detail: The franchisor markets 100+ signed/area-development units against roughly 59 open US locations, meaning most projected growth depends on multi-unit developers actually building out commitments rather than proven openings.
riskFlags.source: Restaurant Dive franchise spotlight; SHARPSHEETS FDD analysis

riskFlags.title: Wide build-cost spread by format (up to ~$1.34M for a biergarten)
riskFlags.detail: Item 7 ranges run from roughly $99,612-$212,900 for a remote/ghost kitchen to $357,437-$625,800 for a fast-casual restaurant and $668,412-$1,340,300 for a full biergarten - a large dispersion that materially changes the payback math depending on format chosen.
riskFlags.source: SHARPSHEETS Dog Haus FDD analysis (2025 FDD)

liquidCapitalMin.source: Vetted Biz - Dog Haus Franchise Insights

totalInvestmentMin.source: SHARPSHEETS Dog Haus FDD analysis (2025 FDD)
totalInvestmentMin.note: Low end is a remote/ghost kitchen ($99,612-$212,900); fast-casual restaurant $357,437-$625,800; full biergarten $668,412-$1,340,300.

totalInvestmentMax.source: SHARPSHEETS Dog Haus FDD analysis (2025 FDD)
totalInvestmentMax.note: High end is a full biergarten format.

isGrowing.note: Active multi-state expansion (OH, AR, AL, TX area development agreements) with a 100+ unit pipeline.

brands.name: Newk's Eatery
brands.notes: Oxford, MS-founded fast-casual cafe (salads, sandwiches, soups, pizza; founded 2004 by Don and Chris Newcomb, franchising 2005), concentrated in the Southeast. Now owned by FSC Franchise Co. (CapitalSpring-backed parent of Beef 'O' Brady's and The Brass Tap) after a 2023 acquisition; FSC is actively ramping Newk's franchising (targeting 10-15 new units/year). Strong AUV (~$2.3M) but the system contracted post-COVID.

riskFlags.title: System contracted sharply post-COVID (122 to ~100 units)
riskFlags.detail: Per the franchise disclosure document, Newk's unit count fell from about 122 units at the start of 2020 to roughly 100 by the end of 2022, a meaningful contraction; new owner FSC is now working to reverse the trend with 10-15 targeted openings per year.
riskFlags.source: Restaurant Dive - How Newk's will help FSC become a billion-dollar platform

riskFlags.title: Two private-equity ownership changes in a decade
riskFlags.detail: Newk's was acquired by Sentinel Capital Partners in 2014 and then sold in 2023 to FSC Franchise Co., a CapitalSpring-backed multi-brand platform. Repeated PE ownership transitions can shift development priorities and support resources for existing franchisees.
riskFlags.source: Restaurant Business; Restaurant Dive (FSC acquisition coverage)

riskFlags.title: Heavy capital requirement: ~$1.5M net worth, $200K+ liquid, $1M+ build
riskFlags.detail: Prospective franchisees need roughly $200,000-$225,000 liquid capital and a minimum net worth around $1,500,000, with total build investment of ~$1.02M-$1.41M, placing Newk's among the more capital-intensive fast-casual concepts.
riskFlags.source: Vetted Biz; SHARPSHEETS / Franchise Gator Newk's FDD summaries

liquidCapitalMin.source: Vetted Biz - Newk's Eatery Franchise Insights

netWorthMin.source: Franchise Gator / SHARPSHEETS Newk's FDD summaries

brands.name: SafeSplash Swim School
brands.notes: Year-round learn-to-swim franchise (the flagship brand of Streamline Brands, which rebranded the whole portfolio to SafeSplash and also operates SwimLabs and Swimtastic). Two very different entry paths: a low-cost 'Hosted' model that rents pool time inside existing facilities (~$48K-$81K) and a capital-heavy 'Dedicated' build-out model (~$945K-$2.1M). Majority owned by PE firm Quad Partners. Actively franchising in the US.

riskFlags.title: Optional 'Enhanced Services' model nearly triples the effective royalty
riskFlags.detail: Standard fees are a 6% royalty + 2% national marketing on gross sales, but the franchisor offers an Enhanced Services Model totaling 16% of gross sales (6% royalty + a 10% enhanced-services fee) - a large recurring cost difference that materially changes unit economics depending on which path the franchisee selects.
riskFlags.source: SHARPSHEETS SafeSplash FDD analysis (2024 FDD)

riskFlags.title: Dedicated-build path requires up to ~$2.1M with no Item 19 in some FDD analyses
riskFlags.detail: The Dedicated (build) and Dual-Brand models run roughly $945K-$2.1M, yet multiple FDD-tracking services report SafeSplash makes no Item 19 financial performance representation - prospective franchisees committing to a multi-million-dollar build have no franchisor-published earnings figures to underwrite against.
riskFlags.source: FranchiseOverview SafeSplash FDD summary (2024 FDD)

riskFlags.title: Private-equity-owned multi-brand roll-up
riskFlags.detail: Streamline Brands (now branded SafeSplash) grew by acquiring Swimtastic (2015), SwimLabs (2017), and Saf-T-Swim, and is majority owned by Quad Partners; franchisees are one brand within a PE-backed portfolio whose capital-allocation and consolidation decisions they do not control.
riskFlags.source: Franchise Times - Streamline Brands consolidates portfolio, rebrands to SafeSplash; PRNewswire

totalInvestmentMin.note: Low end reflects the Hosted (rental) single-brand model; SHARPSHEETS cites $57,500-$81,000 for Hosted, $944,950-$1,327,700 for Dedicated build, and $1,112,000-$1,870,000 for Dual-Brand.

totalInvestmentMax.note: High end is a Dedicated/Dual-Brand build-out.

franchiseFee.note: Varies by model: Hosted $35,000-$37,500; Dedicated single-brand $50,000-$55,000; Dual-brand $60,000.

royaltyPercent.note: 6% of gross sales standard; optional Enhanced Services Model raises total to 16% (6% + 10%).

adFundPercent.note: 2% national marketing fee on gross sales.

totalUnits.note: 110 franchised + 11 company-owned; some directories cite up to 186 across all Streamline brands.

yearFounded.source: Franchising.com Streamline Brands overview; SafeSplash history (founder Matt Lane)

fddAvailable.source: FranchiseOverview; SHARPSHEETS 2024 FDD analyses; The FDD Exchange

item19Available.source: FranchiseOverview SafeSplash FDD summary (no Item 19 representation)
item19Available.note: FDD-tracking services report no franchisor Item 19; a separate directory cited a ~$1.27M average gross revenue figure that could not be confirmed to an FDD Item 19, so AUV is omitted here.

brands.name: Eye Level Learning Centers
brands.notes: Supplemental math and English (reading/writing) tutoring franchise operated in the US by Daekyo America, the US arm of South Korea's Daekyo Co. (founded 1976, US franchising since 1991). Self-directed workbook-based learning model with a low entry cost (~$55K-$129K, $10,000 franchise fee). Royalty is charged per enrolled subject-student rather than as a percent of sales. ~118 US franchised centers; actively recruiting.

riskFlags.title: Modest unit economics: ~$439K average gross sales, multi-year payback
riskFlags.detail: FDD-derived Item 19 data shows average yearly gross sales of about $438,763 with owner-operator estimated earnings of roughly $52,652-$65,815 and an estimated 2.1-4.1 year payback - a low-ceiling, owner-operator income model rather than a scalable high-margin business.
riskFlags.source: Vetted Biz - Eye Level Learning Centers FDD analysis

riskFlags.title: Per-subject royalty structure (not a flat percentage)
riskFlags.detail: Eye Level charges royalties per enrolled subject-student - roughly $36/subject during a probationary period and $32/subject afterward (with directory summaries also citing an effective ~15% of gross revenue plus 2% marketing) - so fees scale with enrollment volume and can be opaque to model versus a simple sales percentage.
riskFlags.source: Franchise Gator / SHARPSHEETS Eye Level FDD summaries

riskFlags.title: Litigation and/or bankruptcy disclosed in FDD
riskFlags.detail: FDD-tracking services indicate Eye Level (Daekyo America) discloses lawsuit and/or bankruptcy information in its FDD Items 3/4 - prospective franchisees should review those items directly for specifics before signing.
riskFlags.source: Vetted Biz - Eye Level Learning Centers FDD analysis

liquidCapitalMin.source: Franchise Gator Eye Level FDD summary
liquidCapitalMin.note: Vetted Biz cites a lower $15,000 minimum cash figure; directories cite $75,000 liquid / $150,000 net worth.

brands.name: Discovery Point
brands.notes: Georgia-based childcare and early-education center franchise (founded 1988, franchising since 1990), ~44 units concentrated in the Southeast. Distinctive flat-fee model: royalties are a fixed monthly dollar amount (~$4,700-$6,000/month) rather than a percentage of sales, plus a flat ~$1,800/month advertising fee. Turnkey development package (site selection, construction, licensing). Actively recruiting US franchisees.

riskFlags.title: Real-estate-heavy investment up to ~$3.3M
riskFlags.detail: Total investment ranges from roughly $369,000 to $3,344,935 depending heavily on land and building - childcare centers require large, licensed, purpose-built facilities, so the high end carries substantial real-estate and construction risk relative to the franchise itself.
riskFlags.source: Franchise Gator - Discovery Point franchise profile

riskFlags.title: Fixed flat-fee royalty is owed regardless of revenue
riskFlags.detail: Discovery Point's defining feature - a flat monthly royalty (~$4,700-$6,000/month) instead of a percentage of sales - benefits high-volume centers but becomes a heavier proportional burden for a center that is ramping up or underperforming, since the fee does not flex down with lower sales.
riskFlags.source: The Franchise Mall; Franchise Help Discovery Point profiles

riskFlags.title: Regional, slow-growth system (~44 units)
riskFlags.detail: After more than 30 years of franchising the system has roughly 44 units concentrated in Georgia and the Southeast - a small, geographically narrow footprint that limits brand recognition for franchisees opening in new markets.
riskFlags.source: Topfranchise / Franchise Help Discovery Point profiles

liquidCapitalMin.source: Franchise Gator - Discovery Point franchise profile

netWorthMin.source: Franchise Gator - Discovery Point franchise profile

totalInvestmentMin.source: Franchise Gator - Discovery Point franchise profile
totalInvestmentMin.note: Topfranchise cites a $405,570 starting figure; high end ~$3,344,935 with land/building.

brands.name: Creative World School
brands.notes: Premium large-format early-education and childcare franchise (founded 1971, franchising since 2000), ~24-29 US units. Among the most capital-intensive childcare concepts: total investment runs into the multiple millions for the purpose-built campus model, with strong Item 19 AUVs (~$2.47M) for mature centers. Actively recruiting US franchisees; requires ~$1M net worth.

riskFlags.title: Extremely high investment (multi-million-dollar campus build)
riskFlags.detail: Total investment is reported in the range of roughly $4.8M-$10.1M depending on land and construction (FDD-derived sources vary), driven by the brand's large purpose-built campus format - one of the highest capital requirements in the childcare franchise category, concentrating risk in real estate and build-out.
riskFlags.source: SHARPSHEETS / Vetted Biz Creative World School FDD analyses (2025 FDD)

riskFlags.title: Small system (~24-29 units) after two decades of franchising
riskFlags.detail: Despite franchising since 2000, Creative World School has only about 24-29 US locations - a slow build reflecting the difficulty and capital intensity of opening each large campus, which means limited brand awareness in new markets.
riskFlags.source: Vetted Biz - Creative World School Franchise Insights

riskFlags.title: Escalating royalty (5% to 7%) plus a high net-worth bar
riskFlags.detail: Royalties start at 5% and step up to a 7% cap after roughly two years of operation, on top of a 2% marketing contribution, and the franchisor requires about $1,000,000 net worth and $350,000+ liquid capital - a high financial qualification bar.
riskFlags.source: SHARPSHEETS Creative World School FDD analysis (2025 FDD)

brands.name: Celebree School
brands.notes: Maryland-based early-childhood education and childcare franchise (infant through school-age) with roughly 29 franchised + 26 company-owned locations, expanding via franchising. Large purpose-built-center model with high capital requirements. Note: the franchisor does NOT make an Item 19 financial performance representation, so no franchisor-published earnings figures exist for due diligence.

riskFlags.title: No Item 19 financial performance representation
riskFlags.detail: Celebree School makes no Item 19 financial performance representation in its FDD - prospective franchisees committing roughly $923K-$5.6M get no franchisor-published revenue, profit, or earnings figures and must build projections independently.
riskFlags.source: FranchiseOverview Celebree Schools FDD summary; SHARPSHEETS

riskFlags.title: Wide, real-estate-driven investment up to ~$5.6M
riskFlags.detail: Total investment ranges from about $923,000 to $5,625,000 depending on land and construction of the purpose-built center - a very wide range where the high end carries substantial real-estate and build-out risk independent of the franchise system itself.
riskFlags.source: SHARPSHEETS Celebree School FDD analysis (2025 FDD)

riskFlags.title: Young franchise system with significant company-owned presence
riskFlags.detail: Of roughly 55 total units, about 26 are company-owned versus 29 franchised, meaning nearly half the system is operated by the franchisor and the franchised cohort is relatively young - limited operating history for franchisee benchmarking.
riskFlags.source: Vetted Biz; FranchiseOverview Celebree School FDD summaries

brands.name: Kumon
brands.notes: The largest after-school math and reading enrichment franchise in the world (Japanese-founded 1958, US locations since 1983), with roughly 1,670+ US franchised centers - by far the biggest unit count in this batch. Self-learning worksheet method. Distinctive economics: royalty is a flat per-student-per-subject monthly fee (not a percent of sales), and the franchisor makes no Item 19 disclosure. Low entry cost; instructor must be the owner-operator.

riskFlags.title: No Item 19 financial performance representation
riskFlags.detail: Kumon does not include an Item 19 in its FDD, so the franchisor publishes no revenue or earnings data for prospective franchisees - notable for a system of 1,600+ US centers where wide per-center performance variation is likely.
riskFlags.source: SHARPSHEETS Kumon FDD analysis (2025 FDD)

riskFlags.title: Per-student royalty (~$38/student/subject/month) scales with enrollment
riskFlags.detail: Royalties are charged as a flat ~$38 per student per subject per month rather than a percentage of revenue, so a math-and-reading student costs ~$76/month in royalties. This fee accrues per enrollment regardless of whether tuition is collected, pressuring margins at low utilization.
riskFlags.source: SHARPSHEETS Kumon FDD analysis (2025 FDD)

riskFlags.title: Owner must serve as the center instructor (limited absentee path)
riskFlags.detail: Kumon's model requires the franchisee to be the trained, hands-on instructor running the center, which constrains multi-unit scaling and absentee ownership relative to percentage-royalty service franchises.
riskFlags.source: Vetted Biz / Franchise Direct Kumon FDD analyses

royaltyPercent.note: Not a percentage: flat ~$38 per student per subject per month (~$76 for a math+reading student). Set to 0 here because there is no percentage-of-sales royalty.

totalUnits.source: SHARPSHEETS / Vetted Biz Kumon FDD analyses (2025 FDD)

absenteeOwnership.source: Vetted Biz / Franchise Direct Kumon FDD analyses (owner is the instructor)

internationalAvailability.source: Kumon corporate (operates across many countries worldwide)

isGrowing.source: Vetted Biz / SHARPSHEETS Kumon FDD analyses
isGrowing.note: Large, mature, steadily expanding US network.

fddAvailable.source: SHARPSHEETS / Franchise Direct / The FDD Exchange

item19Available.source: SHARPSHEETS Kumon FDD analysis (no Item 19 representation)

brands.name: Tutor Doctor
brands.notes: Home-based, mobile in-home and online one-to-one tutoring franchise (founded 2000, franchising in the early-to-mid 2000s), ~340+ US franchised units (600+ globally), zero company-owned. Low-overhead model with no commercial lease - the franchise fee is the largest single startup line item. Actively recruiting US franchisees.

riskFlags.title: Stacked 10% ongoing fees (8% royalty + 2% ad fund)
riskFlags.detail: Tutor Doctor charges an 8% royalty on gross revenue plus a 2% advertising-fund contribution - 10% combined ongoing fees on a low-margin, labor-pass-through tutoring model where much of revenue is paid out to tutors.
riskFlags.source: Vetted Biz / Franchise Direct Tutor Doctor FDD analyses

riskFlags.title: Franchisee litigation alleging fraud/misrepresentation (withdrawn 2013)
riskFlags.detail: A former franchisee filed Restivo v. Tutor Doctor Systems, Inc. (Orange County, CA Superior Court, filed Dec. 12, 2012) alleging breach of contract, fraud, negligent misrepresentation, violation of California's Franchise Investment Law, and unfair trade practices. The claim was withdrawn in July 2013.
riskFlags.source: FranchiseVerdict - Tutor Doctor litigation

riskFlags.title: AUV not disclosed in FDD
riskFlags.detail: Per FDD-tracking services, Tutor Doctor's average unit volume/revenue is undisclosed, so prospective franchisees lack a franchisor-published earnings benchmark for the system.
riskFlags.source: Vetted Biz - Tutor Doctor Franchise Insights

brands.name: Best in Class Education Center
brands.notes: Supplemental math and English enrichment franchise (founded 1995, franchising since 2010), ~36-37 US franchised centers across roughly 9 states. Low entry cost but modest center economics. Protected territories defined by student population (15K-18K students ages 5-17). Actively recruiting US franchisees.

riskFlags.title: High percentage royalty (12% of gross revenues)
riskFlags.detail: The royalty is the greater of $250/month or 12% of monthly gross and affiliate-program revenues (plus 6% of special-program revenues) - a 12% headline royalty is steep for a supplemental-education center with modest unit volumes.
riskFlags.source: Franchise Chatter FDD fee analysis; Vetted Biz

riskFlags.title: Low unit volume and long payback
riskFlags.detail: FDD-derived Item 19 data shows roughly $219,476 average yearly gross sales with owner-operator estimated earnings of only ~$26,338-$32,922 and an estimated 4.3-6.3 year payback - a low-income, slow-return owner-operator model.
riskFlags.source: Vetted Biz - Best in Class Education Center FDD analysis (2025 FDD)

riskFlags.title: Small, geographically narrow system (~9 states)
riskFlags.detail: With about 36-37 centers in roughly 9 states after more than a decade of franchising, brand awareness is limited and franchisees in new markets build recognition largely from scratch.
riskFlags.source: Franchise Chatter / Vetted Biz Best in Class FDD analyses (2025 FDD)

brands.name: Brain Balance
brands.notes: Drug-free, non-medical cognitive/behavioral program for children with learning and attention challenges (ADHD, dyslexia, processing disorders), founded 2006 by Dr. Robert Melillo, franchising since 2008. Roughly 65 all-franchised US centers (no company-owned). Standard and lower-cost satellite formats. The defining buyer concern is multi-year unit contraction plus very heavy mandatory advertising obligations.

riskFlags.title: Three straight years of unit contraction (82 to 65)
riskFlags.detail: Per the 2026 FDD outlet tables, franchised centers fell from 82 (start 2022) to 74, then 69, then 65 by end of 2024 - net losses of -8, -5, and -4 over three consecutive years, with zero company-owned units to offset the decline.
riskFlags.source: Brain Balance 2026 FDD outlet tables (via Franchise Chatter review)

riskFlags.title: Very heavy mandatory advertising: 9% local + 2% national
riskFlags.detail: Beyond the 8% royalty (min $1,000/month), the FDD requires local advertising of 9% of anticipated gross revenue (min $6,000/month) plus a 2% national ad fund (min $200/month) - combined ad obligations can approach or exceed the royalty itself, a fixed cash burden regardless of enrollment.
riskFlags.source: Brain Balance 2026 FDD (via Franchise Chatter review)

riskFlags.title: Wide performance dispersion (Item 19 range $187K-$2.43M)
riskFlags.detail: 2024 Item 19: 61 full-year centers averaged $686,778 gross sales (median $558,573) with a range of $187,073 to $2,430,160 - a roughly 13x spread between weakest and strongest, and the FDD discloses revenue only (no expenses or profit).
riskFlags.source: Brain Balance 2026 FDD Item 19 (via Franchise Chatter review)

adFundPercent.note: 2% national ad fund (min $200/month); PLUS a separate 9% local advertising requirement (min $6,000/month).

item19Available.source: Brain Balance 2026 FDD Item 19 (via Franchise Chatter review)

brands.name: LearningRx
brands.notes: One-on-one cognitive skills 'brain training' franchise (founded 2002 by Dr. Ken Gibson in Colorado Springs; franchising from 2003), roughly 47 centers (46 franchised + 1 company-owned). Distinct from tutoring - the program targets underlying cognitive skills. The single most important due-diligence item is the brand's 2016 FTC deceptive-advertising settlement, which constrains the claims franchisees may make.

riskFlags.title: FTC deceptive-advertising settlement (2016, $200,000)
riskFlags.detail: The FTC charged LearningRx Franchise Corp. and CEO Dr. Ken Gibson with deceptively claiming the brain-training programs were clinically proven to permanently improve serious conditions (ADHD, autism, dementia, Alzheimer's, stroke, concussion) and to substantially raise grades, test scores, and earnings. The 2016 settlement barred those unsubstantiated claims and imposed a $200,000 payment - directly limiting franchisees' marketing claims.
riskFlags.source: Federal Trade Commission press release

riskFlags.title: Escalating-then-tiered royalty plus capped marketing fund
riskFlags.detail: Royalty is 8% of gross revenues, stepping down to 7.5% above $500,000 and 7% above $750,000 annually, plus a 3% Marketing Development Fund capped at $15,000/center/year - a relatively heavy ongoing-fee load for a small-footprint service center.
riskFlags.source: SHARPSHEETS LearningRx FDD analysis (2025 FDD)

riskFlags.title: Small system (~47 centers) and modest AUV
riskFlags.detail: The system is roughly 47 centers (46 franchised, 1 company-owned) with FDD-derived average sales around $375,000 - a small, owner-operator-scale network where brand reach and unit volumes are limited.
riskFlags.source: Franchise Chatter LearningRx FDD review; Vetted Biz

royaltyPercent.note: 8% of gross revenues; steps down to 7.5% above $500K and 7% above $750K annually.

avgUnitRevenue.note: Approximate average center revenue per FDD-derived analysis.

totalUnits.source: SHARPSHEETS / Vetted Biz LearningRx FDD analyses (2025 FDD)

yearFounded.source: Wikipedia; LearningRx corporate history (Dr. Ken Gibson, Colorado Springs)

item19Available.source: Franchise Chatter LearningRx FDD review (revenue/expense Item 19)

brands.name: Club Z Tutoring
brands.notes: In-home and online one-to-one tutoring franchise (Tampa, FL; founded 1995, franchising since 1998), home-based with no commercial real estate. Once one of the largest tutoring networks (~328 US franchised units, 0 company-owned), but the system has been contracting for several years. Low entry cost. Actively recruiting US franchisees.

riskFlags.title: Multi-year unit contraction (362 to 328)
riskFlags.detail: Per the 2025/2026 FDD outlet tables, franchised units fell from 362 (end 2022) to 347 (2023, -21 net) to 328 (2024, -19 net) - two consecutive years of double-digit net losses, with no company-owned units to offset the decline.
riskFlags.source: Club Z! 2026 FDD outlet tables (via Franchise Chatter review)

riskFlags.title: Royalty with monthly minimums regardless of revenue
riskFlags.detail: Royalty is the greater of 6-8% of gross sales (by territory size) or $550/month, plus marketing of the greater of 2% or $50/month - the dollar minimums create a fixed monthly floor that pressures low-volume or ramping franchisees.
riskFlags.source: Club Z! 2026 FDD (via Franchise Chatter review)

riskFlags.title: Item 19 reports activity metrics, not unit revenue
riskFlags.detail: The 2024 Item 19 discloses operational figures (average private-student counts by hours worked, tutor pay $24-$28/hr, client rates $52-$125/hr) rather than a clean average unit revenue, making it harder for prospects to model expected center sales directly.
riskFlags.source: Club Z! 2026 FDD Item 19 (via Franchise Chatter review)

brands.name: Venture X
brands.notes: Upscale, hospitality-driven coworking and flexible-workspace franchise (founded 2012, franchising since ~2016 under United Franchise Group / Vast Coworking), roughly 44 US locations (100+ globally). Capital-intensive build-out with a long-term commercial lease at its core. Actively recruiting US franchisees; 2024 system sales reportedly grew ~45% to ~$59M.

riskFlags.title: Capital-intensive build tied to a long commercial lease in a volatile sector
riskFlags.detail: Total investment runs roughly $341,000 to $3,273,000, anchored by a large leased office build-out. The flexible-workspace category carries real demand risk: WeWork's bankruptcy, hybrid-work uncertainty, potential oversupply in mature markets, and interest-rate sensitivity can all depress occupancy and rents that the franchisee remains liable for.
riskFlags.source: SHARPSHEETS Venture X FDD analysis; Allwork.space coworking 2026 trends

riskFlags.title: Long ramp and high franchise fee under a multi-brand franchisor platform
riskFlags.detail: The initial franchise fee is roughly $79,500 and Venture X sits within United Franchise Group / Vast Coworking, a large multi-brand franchisor (led by Ray Titus) that sets development priorities across many brands; coworking centers also take time to reach mature occupancy, lengthening the path to breakeven.
riskFlags.source: SHARPSHEETS Venture X FDD analysis; United Franchise Group / Vast Coworking

riskFlags.title: Relatively young, modest US footprint (~44 locations)
riskFlags.detail: With about 44 US locations (100+ globally) since beginning to franchise around 2016, the US system is still emerging; franchisees in newer markets build brand awareness for a premium-priced offering against established and discount coworking competitors.
riskFlags.source: SHARPSHEETS Venture X FDD analysis; Franchise Times emerging-franchise coverage

totalInvestmentMin.note: Franchisor cites a starting figure around $340,750 before concessions/financing.
